other_observers: William Burnidge, plus 6 other folks whose first names I wrote down, but not their last names. Their first names, for what it's worth, were David & Katie, Mike & Steve, and Audrey & Bob.; Sort of. One of the participants in the outing was the first to see the bird. She quickly recognized it as a vireo, and then got me on the bird. Then I tentatively ID'd it, and got others on the bird. Then we all worked through the ID, and arriv
duration: 2 minutes (plus 30 minutes searching in vain after initial sighting)
description: It was definitely a "Solitary Vireo." As it was one of the more brightly colored species (Cassin's or Blue-headed), it was of immediate interest, as either of the brightly colored species is unusual in far eastern Colorado in spring.  The bird was brightly colored and brightly patterned overall: blue face with white spectacles: greenish-yellow above, yellowish on the flanks, and white below. In my field notebook, which I had with me at the time, I drew a very crude rendering of the face, coarsely indicating the good contrast between the bird's dark face and pale throat, i.e., sharp malar contrast.Behaviors: Vireo-like, the bird moved slowly in the emerging vegetation in the tall Siberian Elms at the headquarters of the Fox Ranch. We saw it in and out of the leaves for about 2 minutes. Then, despite constant searching off and on for 30 minutes, we could not relocate it. Nobody saw it fly off, but it may have. Or maybe it simply stayed out of view somehow. The bird never vocalized. We saw it snag or at least attempt to snag several caterpillars (probably family Geometridae).Call: not heardPlumage: probably definitive basic
